Performance data quoted represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the figures shown. Principal value and investment returns will fluctuate, and investors’ shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost. Performance would have been lower if fees had not been waived in various periods. Total returns assume the reinvestment of all distributions and the deduction of all fund expenses. Returns with sales charge reflect a deduction of the stated maximum sales charge. An investor cannot invest directly in an index, and unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expenses or sales charges. Returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized. All classes of shares may not be available to all investors or through all distribution channels. For current month-end performance, please visit franklintempleton.com.

The fund began offering Advisor Class shares on 12/31/1996 and the fund began offering A Class shares on 9/10/2018. Performance quotations have been calculated as follows: (a) for Advisor Class periods prior to 12/31/1996, a restated figure is used based on the fund’s Class A1 performance; for A Class periods prior to 9/10/2018, a restated 1gure is used based on the fund’s Class A1 performance. The performance was adjusted to take into account differences in class-specific operating expenses and maximum sales charges. (b) For periods after share class offering, performance for the specific share class is used, reflecting the expenses and maximum sales charges applicable to that class.

Gross expenses are the fund’s total annual operating expenses as of the fund’s prospectus available at the time of publication. Actual expenses may be higher and may impact portfolio returns. Net expenses reflect contractual fee waivers, expense caps and/or reimbursements, which cannot be terminated prior to 01/31/2026 without Board consent. Additional amounts may be voluntarily waived and/or reimbursed and may be modified or discontinued at any time without notice.

What are the Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. Low-rated, high-yield bonds are subject to greater price volatility, illiquidity and possibility of default. Fixed income securities involve interest rate, credit, inflation and reinvestment risks, and possible loss of principal. As interest rates rise, the value of fixed income securities falls. Changes in the credit rating of a bond, or in the credit rating or financial strength of a bond’s issuer, insurer or guarantor, may affect the bond’s value. Equity securities are subject to price fluctuation and possible loss of principal. International investments are subject to special risks, including currency fluctuations and social, economic and political uncertainties, which could increase volatility. These risks are magnified in emerging markets. The manager may consider environmental, social and governance (ESG) criteria in the research or investment process; however, ESG considerations may not be a determinative factor in security selection. In addition, the manager may not assess every investment for ESG criteria, and not every ESG factor may be identified or evaluated. These and other risks are discussed in the fund’s prospectus.

Glossary

Nasdaq Composite Index is a market-capitalization-weighted index that is designed to represent the performance of NASDAQ securities and includes over 3,000 stocks. Source: Nasdaq OMX. The S&P 500 Index features 500 leading U.S. publicly traded companies, with a primary emphasis on market capitalization. Source: © S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC. All rights reserved.

Important Information

The information provided is not a complete analysis of every material fact regarding any country, market, industry, security or fund. Because market and economic conditions are subject to change, comments, opinions and analyses are rendered as of the date of this material and may change without notice. A portfolio manager’s assessment of a particular security, investment or strategy is not intended as individual investment advice or a recommendation or solicitation to buy, sell or hold any security or to adopt any investment strategy; it is intended only to provide insight into the fund’s portfolio selection process. Holdings are subject to change.

The Benchmark: 50% USA High Div Yield + 25% High Yield Very Liquid + 25% US Agg Index is composed of the Blended 50% MSCI USA High Dividend Yield Index + 25% Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index + 25% Bloomberg US Aggregate Index. The MSCI USA High Dividend Yield Index is designed to reflect the performance of mid- and large-cap equities (excluding REITs) with higher dividend income, which is sustainable and persistent, than average dividend yields of securities in the MSCI USA Index. The Bloomberg US High Yield Very Liquid Index (VLI) is a component of the US Corporate High Yield Index designed to track a more liquid component of the U.S. dollar-denominated, high-yield, fixed-rate corporate bond market. The Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index is comprised of investment-grade, U.S. dollar-denominated government, corporate, and mortgage- and asset-backed issues having at least one year to maturity. Source: MSCI makes no warranties and shall have no liability with respect to any MSCI data reproduced herein. No further redistribution or use is permitted. This report is not prepared or endorsed by MSCI. Bloomberg Indices.

Important data provider notices and terms available at www.franklintempletondatasources.com. All data is subject to change.

The 30-day SEC yield is calculated using the net income (interest and dividends) per share earned over a trailing 30-day period (annualized), divided by the fund’s share price at the end of that period. It may not equal the fund’s actual income distribution rate, which reflects the fund’s past dividends paid to shareholders.

Franklin Distributors, LLC. Member FINRA/SIPC.

© Franklin Templeton. All rights reserved.

Before investing, carefully consider a fund’s investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses. You can find this and other information in each prospectus, or summary prospectus, if available, at www.franklintempleton.com. Please read it carefully.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: UGI: Buy This Undervalued Utility With Data Center Catalysts
data.description:
One sector that’s done well over the past 12 months is data storage and memory chips, which is comprised of players like Micron Technology (MU), Seagate (STX), and Western Digital (WDC). These are AI-adjacent players that are well-positioned to benefit from surging demand for AI applications. Investing in pick-and-shovel plays can be highly profitable, especially when they are fairly or attractively priced.

This brings me to UGI Corp. (UGI), which has data center catalysts of its own. I last covered UGI in October, highlighting its AmeriGas turnaround, disciplined capital allocation, and focus on regulated utility investments. UGI has since produced a robust 18.8% total return since my last piece, far outpacing the 1.7% rise in the S&P 500 (SPY) over the same timeframe.

Despite the recent uptick in price to $38.10, UGI remains below where it traded in 2021, with a very reasonable forward P/E of 12.2 and a 3.9% dividend yield, as shown below.

In this article, I revisit UGI including recent business results, and discuss why the stock is still a bargain for long-term income and total return potential, so let’s get started!

Why UGI?

UGI Corporation is a diversified energy distribution and marketing company with a history that dates back over a century. Through its subsidiaries, UGI distributes, stores, transports, and markets a broad suite of energy products. This includes natural gas, electricity, propane and other liquefied petroleum gases (LPG) across the U.S. and Europe.

In recent years, UGI has been investing in natural gas infrastructure upgrades to meet growing demand for electric generation. These projects are aimed at enhancing delivery reliability and system safety in UGI’s core markets. It’s also focused on portfolio optimization by divesting non-core LPG businesses over the past year to sharpen focus on gas system upgrades and new LNG and RNG facilities.

UGI recently demonstrated strong fiscal year 2025 results (ended September 30th), with adjusted EPS growing by 8.5% YoY to $3.32, surpassing the previous guidance range of $3.00 to $3.15. This was driven by outperformance across multiple segments. As shown below, Midstream and Marketing and the AmeriGas Propane segments were the drivers behind the increased bottom line, more than offsetting declines in Utilities and the International segment.

Notably, AmeriGas was a standout performer as its turnaround gained steam throughout the year, with segment EBIT rising by 17% over the prior year period. This was driven by cost reductions that improved profitability. Actions that enhanced margins for AmeriGas included an exit from a breakeven wholesale business in order to sharpen focus on profitable customers. Plus, improved supply and logistics practices that included route optimization technologies resulted in a 10% fuel cost savings on pilot operations.

Moreover, the utilities segment achieved record EBIT of $403 million for the year. This was driven by 10% increase in core volumes from colder weather and gas base rate increases (notably in West Virginia). The utilities segment also added 11,500 residential and commercial customers. The Midstream & Marketing segment saw increased margins from gas marketing, and an increase in investment tax credits associated with newly operational RNG facilities. This helped to offset lower margins from gas gathering and processing.

Management is guiding for 5-7% EBIT growth in FY 2026, with expectations for higher gas rates in Pennsylvania, natural gas business growth, and customer growth and retention at AmeriGas. These catalysts are offset by a reduction in favorable tax impacts in 2026 due to lower tax credits associated with RNG projects, as most of the investments were made in FY 2025.

Nonetheless, North America is the largest market for RNG, and is expected to grow at a double-digit CAGR as methane from waste is injected into gas grids. As shown below, landfill and animal manure waste are the biggest growth drivers for RNG.

UGI plans to continue to modernize its infrastructure, such as replacing 130 miles of pipelines in utility systems. These investments support expected robust base rate growth of 9% or higher this year. Moreover, UGI has growth catalysts stemming from data centers, with over 50 agreements with counterparties at present, as noted during the Q&A session of the last earnings call:

Q: Last quarter, Bob, you mentioned all the NDAs that you had signed around some of the activity happening in your backyard maybe if you can get an update on that.

A: Yes, we still continue to see a lot of activity even more so than when we last spoke about it. We've advanced some of the projects with some interested parties. We have NDA so we can't necessarily go into it. But again, the amount of NDAs that we have with counterparties is north of 50. I mean, we've got significant discussions underway and in various stages with the various counterparties. So these things take time, but we are definitely keenly focused on it and looking to be part of all the growth that we're expected to see in Pennsylvania.

UGI maintains a solid balance sheet with a safe net debt to adjusted EBITDA ratio of 3.9x. Management intends to keep the leverage ratio at or below 4x, and UGI currently has $1.6 billion of liquidity. This lends support to the 3.9% dividend yield, which is well-covered by a 45% payout ratio. UGI has paid an uninterrupted dividend for 37 years.

Lastly, I continue to see value in UGI at the current price of $38.10 with a forward P/E of 12.2, which sits below it historical P/E of 14.7, as shown below.

At the current valuation, it appears the market still isn’t baking in the full potential of UGI’s continued AmeriGas turnaround and growth drivers in the utilities and midstream segments. Analysts estimate 5% to 8% EPS growth annually for fiscal years 2027-2029. This, combined with the near-4% dividend yield and potential for a continued reversion to the mean valuation could result in double-digit total returns in the near term.

Risks to UGI include volatility in weather, which means that warmer-than-expected winters can lead to a reduction in power demand. UGI’s propane and energy marketing segments face commodity price risks, which can compress margins. UGI is also subject to regulatory risks, which include the potential for unfavorable rate case outcomes and/or tighter environmental standards.

Investor Takeaway

UGI Corporation remains an attractive income and total return opportunity despite its recent run. Its stock still trades below historical valuation levels while fundamentals continue to improve. The AmeriGas turnaround is gaining real traction, while regulated utility investments are driving steady rate-base growth. Plus, midstream exposure to RNG and data center demand adds long-term growth drivers that the market appears to be underappreciating.

Lastly, UGI offers a strong combination of stability, income, and upside potential. This is supported by management’s guidance for 5-7% EBIT growth, safe leverage, and a well-covered near-4% dividend. Patient investors are being paid to wait as the company executes on its strategy and valuation gradually normalizes. As such, I maintain a ‘Buy’ rating on UGI.

data.title: Baker Hughes: Why I'm Still Not Buying Despite Strong LNG And Data Center Exposure
data.description:
Introduction

Last time I covered Baker Hughes (BKR), I called them “A Bet On Data Centers, Energy Infrastructure, And LNG,” and even though that’s something I’d gladly get more exposure to since I view it as one of the biggest tailwinds of the upcoming decade, BKR’s valuation already reflected high expectations.

However, even with some solid deals announced recently, a good report released in the meantime and potential for macro improvements, the overall risk-reward still doesn’t justify buying BKR at the current level, with the price reflecting a lot of confidence that the company will have to meet.

Internal Developments

BKR reported a solid Q3 recently, beating the market’s revenue and EPS expectations while also delivering a solid free cash flow, although it fell by ~7% YoY. They also mentioned the strong order momentum continuing, especially in IET (Industrial & Energy Technology) and SSPS (Subsea & Surface Pressure Systems), with $4.1 billion and $1.2 billion in orders, respectively, thanks to LNG equipment demand and large subsea awards in Turkey and Brazil.

Recently, we’ve seen BKR announcing a strategic alliance with Glenfarne to supply equipment for their Alaska LNG project, which is yet another major LNG pipeline that should help the US dominate the industry even better after Russia started its war with Ukraine. They also announced being awarded a multi-year contract with Kuwait Oil Company to provide advanced artificial lift systems and for Kuwait’s oil and gas fields.

This is alongside similar deals such as the ones with Aramco, Turkish Petroleum, or the deal to supply liquefaction equipment for Sempra’s (SRE) Port Arthur Phase 2 project in Texas after already participating in Phase 1 as well. As a note, SRE sold a 45% stake in Sempra Infrastructure Partners to KKR (KKR) and CPP Investments (Canada Pension Plan) for $10 billion recently while also getting a project-level equity financing of roughly $7 billion, basically securing the future of this project, in case it wasn’t certain enough already.

As a note, Chart Industries’ (GTLS) shareholders have previously approved to the $13.6 billion acquisition offer from Baker Hughes, being expected to close in mid-2026 (pending regulatory approval) and improve the company’s presence in high-growth markets such as LNG, data centers, clean energy, etc. This comes following a series of portfolio reshuffling actions, selling their precision sensors and instrumentation product line for $1.06 billion back in mid-2025 and buying Continental Disc for $540 million around the same time.

Regarding their capital allocation framework, BKR highlighted their targets for the post-Chart acquisition deleveraging phase, aiming at 1x to 1.5x Net Debt/LTM aEBITDA within 24 months of the deal’s close. The buybacks would also remain flexible ($0 in Q3), while the dividend will remain at current levels (~2.06% yield today) with prospects of future growth.

Financially, based on BKR’s latest report, we see that they’re still in an excellent position, with the current assets almost covering the company’s current liabilities plus long-term debt, with a still solid ~$2.7 billion cash and cash equivalents position. Again, keep in mind that this will change quite a bit after the Chart Industries deal, as that would increase their debt significantly (net debt would get to around $15.8 billion, depending on how these balance sheets will evolve until closing), although they plan to reduce it, as mentioned.

Macro Developments

Again, I’ll reiterate that oil and gas prices play a crucial role for BKR at the end of the day. Simply put, lower oil prices mean CAPEX cuts for many of BKR’s partners, which hurts the demand for their products directly.

As oil expectations are quite bad in the near future - suggested by the major oversupply expected from 2026 by virtually all major institutions - we’ve seen very dark estimates from J.P. Morgan for example, who sees oil potentially dropping in the $30s by 2027 if this environment continues.

For context, this comes alongside OPEC+ agreeing to another hike in December, but halting its increases in Q1 2026 at least, which could offer significant relief for oil. However, the market expects this halt to be caused by the expectations of very significant oversupply in the near future, with reports of rising US crude inventories.

There’s also been some progress towards a potential peace in Ukraine, with both sides looking into potential amends and Russia claiming that the recent talks were “proceeding constructively” after US negotiators met with Russian officials in Florida. But again, keep in mind that this is not that great for oil prices and the market is already pricing in these developments, as a peace deal could help sanctioned Russian oil, potentially hurting the price.

What such an environment would translate into for a majority of US/EU producers is a significant drop into the negative or barely breaking even, with expansion/drilling/etc. CAPEX being the last thing they’d focus on, meaning less demand for BKR’s products and services.

We’ve also seen activist investor Ananym Capital recommend the spin-off of BKR’s oil services equipment, arguing that this move would unlock a boost in stock price by at least 60%, implying that the market applies a very steep sum of parts valuation discount as most of their 2025 earnings came from the technologies unit. Note that the fund is a very tiny shareholder overall, but BKR represents about 15.06% of their portfolio as of the latest filing.

This move would reduce this exposure to oil prices mentioned above and allow the company to focus on the high-growth segments of the business, fitting the company’s “energy technology” narrative and potentially leading to a higher premium for the market as oil assets will probably be discounted in the near future due to the weak prices.

Recently, Baker Hughes’ CEO discussed the expected impact of Trump’s tariffs, saying that he sees more than $100 million (similar to their April forecast) due to them. However, I’ll mention again that tariffs are likely not here to stay forever, being more of a tool to get leverage in potentially negotiating better trade deals/conditions for the US, so it’s uncertain if this >$100 million cost will persist for long.

He also talked about their plans to accelerate production of their NovaLT turbines (primary manufacturing hub is in Tuscany, Italy - specifically in Florence, Massa and Avenza), with about 70% of the ones sold in H1’25 being used for data centers, so it’s not surprising to see why they’re pushing for this, potentially allowing them to capitalize on the massive AI/data center/infrastructure investing boom in more ways.

BKR also announced a 5-year €300 million investment plan back in October 2025 to improve their Italian production capacity, boosting existing production facilities across the country.

However, the situation in Venezuela could also cause disruptions and even more costs, as a war in the Caribbean can significantly disrupt global trade routes due to the country’s proximity to the Panama Canal, which could even be a target if they want to retaliate since it would cause significant damage to the US at a time with already high uncertainty, causing delays, higher costs, supply chain issues, etc.

Again, it’s not about who would “win” this war from a military point of view - I doubt there’s anything to argue on that side - but the effects can be even more significant especially on the market’s opinions, as oil is among the most volatile/speculative commodities. We’ve seen oil prices jump once again after the US intercepted another oil tanker off Venezuela’s coast, and that’s after several other ups and downs in recent weeks as the situation developed.

Meanwhile, LNG projects continued to advance in North America, with Trump’s stance in regard to the US replacing Russia and dominating the global LNG market being even clearer following the $750 billion US-EU LNG deal.

However, Canada also came with some improvements in this regard - albeit in very early stages - with the Grand Bargain memorandum signed on November 27 attempting to end years of “hostility” between Ottawa and Edmonton by swapping the federal approval for fossil fuel expansion (Canada’s natural gas is at multi-decade lows now, but it’s hard to sell because of their infrastructure) in exchange for provincial cooperation on carbon pricing.

Although this memorandum is seen as a “betrayal” of Canada's climate targets by environmental groups, others see it as a move with potential to “reshape Canada’s energy infrastructure landscape,” as it could revive the West Coast pipeline corridor and finally bypass regulatory deadlocks, allowing companies to gain direct access to the Asian market and sell at better prices instead of having to sell at the “Canadian discount” due to the logistics behind it.

Of course, as an industry leader, BKR would very likely be able to capitalize on this shift in politics in North America, which could potentially generate even more revenue on top of the high-growth areas.

Risks Reiterated

To reiterate, even though BKR has significant long-term potential thanks to their exposure to LNG and AI, oil still plays a very important role for the company and especially their clients, as that's ultimately a major source of capital for them to fund these high-growth projects.

Meanwhile, the company doesn't have the best history of growth, potentially even hurting Chart's otherwise very solid expansion, while the debt this acquisition would require is very significant and could potentially generate a ton of interest, pressuring the company's margins at an uncertain time.

Valuation

Based on recent free cash flows and margins, the combined FCF of the post-acquisition company would be around $2.6 billion (similar to last year’s level, with some growth offset by the interest generated by the new debt they'll have to issue). Again, I’ll add $50 million in synergies per year in 2026 to 2028 (below expectations) on top of a more optimistic CAGR of 7.5% over the first 5 years (similar to the EPS expectations), followed by a still solid 6% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years thanks to the continued excellent demand for their products and services, with a 3.25% terminal growth rate - better than their recent history.

I’ll go again for an 8.5% WACC (assumptions: ~9.5% cost of equity, ~3.5% cost of debt), an estimated net debt following the acquisition of Chart of about $15.8 billion, with $171 million in non-controlling interests and 986.77 million shares outstanding as of the Q3 report.

This way, we get to an enterprise value of $70.94 billion, an equity value of $54.96 billion and an intrinsic value of $55.70 per share combined with today’s ~$44.71.

Conclusions

Overall, I still don’t see the risk-reward for BKR being worth it, even though Chart will be a solid addition to an already solid portfolio. As much as I like the industry and the exposure they offer to it, the price is not leaving that much room for a margin of safety unless we assume optimistic growth rates, well above the company’s not-so-great history.

data.title: Interparfums: Capital-Light Model Positions Stock For Growth Despite Near-Term Headwinds
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Interparfums (IPAR) is a capital-light fragrance licensee with a long history of compounding revenue through brand development and license expansion. Today, the company is facing headwinds from destocking, macro-driven demand softness, and the expiration of the Boucheron license. While licenses are the biggest risk for the company, its business model has been able to maneuver these risks. I see upside in the business due to continued market growth and portfolio diversification; therefore, I am giving Interparfums a Buy rating.

Recent Developments

Interparfums has had a rocky 2025, with the stock down over 35% YTD and more than 15% below its post-Liberation Day lows around $100 per share. Although the shares rallied to $140 in June, they have since reversed sharply and are now trading around $85. Additionally, the company trades at a 9.5x EV/EBITDA, below the sector median of 11x and below Interparfums 3- and 5-year average EV/EBITDA of 12.8x and 14x, respectively. In their Q2 earnings call in August, management cited destocking, slower demand, and tariffs. In Q3, they reduced their 2025 guidance from $1.51 billion in revenue to just $1.47 billion (-2.65%) while slashing EPS from $5.35 to $5.12 (-4.3%). For 2026, they are guiding only 1% revenue growth with EPS of $4.85, citing the macro environment and the expiration of their Boucheron license with foreign exchange gains. It is worth noting that these headwinds are not company-specific, as other industry players are seeing similar slowdowns, but this has likely worried investors, as Interparfums has grown revenue at a 12% CAGR since 2015.

Interparfums' Business

Interparfums sells fragrances with licenses from major fashion brands, including Jimmy Choo, Montblanc, Coach, GUESS, Donna Karan/DKNY, Lacoste, and Ferragamo, among others. These listed brands represent over 70% of Interparfums' sales. Although Interparfums does not own manufacturing facilities, they develop, market, and distribute these fragrances, which they have been doing for over 40 years. Brands partner with Interparfums for another revenue stream and to further their brand recognition in the $57 billion fragrance industry. Additionally, these individual brands may not have the industry experience and network that Interparfums has, which is costly and difficult to develop, so many brands choose to sell these fragrance licenses.

Interparfums operates in a competitive industry, and they face challenges from brands choosing to take fragrances in-house and other licensees like Coty (COTY). In my view, management is critical for this business, as these brands have many options for their fragrances, and they must see significant value in licensing rather than holding it in-house and keeping all the profits. Luckily for Interparfums, their co-founders, Jean Madar and Philippe Benacin, are the Chairman & CEO and Vice Chairman & CEO of Interparfums SA, respectively. This is a huge strength for the business, but the co-founders are in their mid-60s and losing them may harm the business.

Interparfums' business is dependent on maintaining these fragrance licenses and growing the brands, and it is always a risk that they lose some of their major brands, so I compiled a list of their brands dating back to 2014 to see how it has evolved. I denoted brands ending in red, brands starting in green, and one-year brands in yellow. Below is my full compilation.

Interparfums has gained more brands over the years (lost 9 and gained 18); however, even in the years they lost brands (2016 and 2023), revenue was still higher. Losing brands is part of the business, and Interparfums has done a good job of expanding its portfolio. The real growth in their business is in the brands themselves, while adding more to the portfolio is just a bonus. Interparfums will also launch Goutal and Off-White in 2026 and Longchamp in 2027. Finally, Interparfums does not have any core licenses expiring soon (with the exception of Boucheron), so the portfolio should be strong in the medium term.

Interparfums has a good capital-light business in my view, but they have constant pressure from other licensees and brands themselves, so effective management and trust are key to their long-term success.

DCF Valuation for Interparfums

While management has guided for a tough 2026, 2027 should be better with new brands launching. Analysts seem optimistic with a 2027 consensus of $5.64 EPS and $1.61 billion in revenue. In my model, I will take a more cautious approach. On slide 12 of their November investor presentation, Interparfums cited Precedence Research's 6% 10-year luxury fragrance market CAGR. While it is not unreasonable to assume Interparfums will deliver this long-term growth, I will be more conservative and assume a lower 3.25% 10-year revenue CAGR and 2.5% terminally. While this is low if you look at Interparfums' history, I think it is fair given the market growth and competition. I will also assume EBIT margins increase slightly from continued operational improvements. Finally, I am using a 7.88% cost of capital with a 5% cost of debt and an 8.2% cost of equity with a 4.2% risk-free rate (10-year US Treasury), a 0.89 unlevered industry beta (0.94 levered), and a 4.25% equity risk premium due to the international presence.

I got an intrinsic value of $110.58 for Interparfums, representing a 31% undervaluation at current prices. While the company seems significantly undervalued based on its cash flows, I see risk in the long term with licenses. While Interparfums is developing its own brand, Solférino, and has started to acquire brands (like Goutal) rather than licensing them, most of its revenue is still from these uncertain contracts. To stress test, I will assume their two largest brands, Montblanc (15% of sales) and Jimmy Choo (17% of sales), decide not to extend their contracts in 2030 and 2031, respectively. With these direct sales drops, the intrinsic value would still be $83. While it is a risk they lose some licenses, management is moving away from a pure licensing play, and the market is pricing in their biggest brands not renewing.

Risks and Final Thoughts

I want to look at two cases to better quantify this license risk.

First, in 2012, Interparfums lost its Burberry license, which represented almost 50% of its sales. The brand decided to take its operations in-house before re-licensing its fragrances to COTY in 2017. While this had an impact on Interparfums, sales and operating income only declined 23% and 36%, respectively, from 2012 (46% of sales with Burberry) to 2014 (0% of sales with Burberry).

Secondly, COTY held a license to sell Gucci fragrance, but in October, Gucci sold its beauty business to L'Oréal for $4.7 billion, which included the 50-year license deal for Gucci fragrances. Gucci sales were estimated at around $555 million for COTY.

In both of these cases, these large brands decided they could not keep their fragrances in-house, which subsides a risk that when brands become too big, they will take their operations in-house. It is worth noting that some major brands have successfully taken their fragrances in-house, but it is not an easy thing to do. Burberry tried and failed, and Gucci relicensed. Secondly, losing a major brand does not kill the business. Today, Interparfums' biggest contract is Jimmy Choo, which is only 17% of sales compared to 46% from Burberry. Even with the loss, their business model allowed their other brands to succeed, and the impact on the entire company was limited.

With Interparfums' Co-founders still leading the company and their capital-light business model, I have confidence that they can continue to grow and use their strong balance sheet to continue to diversify their portfolio, limiting license risk. While the business has long-term license risk and operates in a competitive industry, the market seems to be overly pessimistic, which gives investors a large margin of safety if the company continues to grow with minimal losses if the company loses some licenses; therefore, I am giving Interparfums a Buy rating.

data.title: Capital One Financial PFDs: What's In Your Portfolio?
data.description:
Introduction

Sorry, I couldn’t resist playing off their credit card tagline. With the FOMC restarting reductions in the FFR, though with three dissenting votes, I’m returning to covering preferred stocks. Especially for fixed-rate issues, rate cuts should bode well for price support for those investments. Here I review all the preferred stocks available from Capital One Financial Corporation (COF). Those issues are:

  • Capital One Financial Corp., 5.00% Dep Shares Non-Cumul Perp Pfd Stock Series I (COF.PR.I).
  • Capital One Financial Corp., 4.80% Dep Shares Non-Cumul Perp Pfd Stock Series J (COF.PR.J).
  • Capital One Financial Corp. 4.625% Dep Shares Non-Cumul Perp Pfd Stock Series K (COF.PR.K).
  • Capital One Financial Corp 4.375% Dep Shares Non-Cumul Perp Pfd Stock Series L (COF.PR.L).
  • Capital One Financial Corp. 4.25% Dep Shares Non-Cumul Perp Pfd Stock Series N (COF.PR.N).

The goal being to decide if one should be part of the income generating part of an investor’s portfolio structure. When compared to several large national banks, these issues provide 50-60bps in extra yield. When taking yield and Call odds into account, I gave my Buy rating to the Series L.

Capital One Financial Corporation review

Any preferred stocks available due diligence process should start by evaluating the issuer to determine how secure the dividend payments are and in case of severe trouble, the ability to make the holders of their preferred stocks “whole.” Seeking Alpha describes this issuer as (edited):

Capital One Financial Corporation operates as the financial services holding company for the Capital One, National Association, which engages in the provision of various financial products and services in the United States, Canada, and the United Kingdom. It operates through three segments: Credit Card, Consumer Banking, and Commercial Banking. The company accepts checking accounts, money market deposits, and time deposits. Its loan products include credit card loans; auto and retail banking loans; and commercial and multifamily real estate, and commercial and industrial loans. The company offers credit and debit card products; online direct banking services. It serves consumers, small businesses, and commercial clients through digital channels, branches, cafés, and other distribution channels located in New York, Louisiana, Texas, Maryland, Virginia, New Jersey, and California. Capital One Financial Corporation was founded in 1988 and is headquartered in McLean, Virginia.

Capital One Financial ranks 6th in size among U.S. based banks with $652B in assets. While they have a limited branch network (referred to as cafes), customers have access to over 70,000 ATMs nationwide.

Last spring, Capital One completed its acquisition of Discover Financial services (press release). While this limits the historical data value, for this first part I chose to include annual data back to 2019. For the first concern, dividend payments, I look at the Income Statement, where we see that the 2024 ratio of Net Income/PFD DVDs was 15.5X.

The merger with Discover Financials probably will change the ratio but that is hard for me to decipher when looking at the Q3 report: here is a link to that quarterly report.

As for the ability to redeem all the preferred stocks, I like seeing how much Total Common Equity, or TCE, is available. Here, we have a ratio of TCE/PFDs of 20X using the latest quarterly data. That is one of the strongest ratios I have seen.

For a more in-depth review of Capital One, another Seeking Alpha contributor gave them a Buy rating in their recent article.

Preferred stocks analysis

Factor Series I Series J Series K Series L Series N
Issued 9/14/19 1/28/20 9/10/20 4/29/21 7/26/21
Size 40MM 50MM 5MM 27MM 17MM
Coupon 5.00% 4.80% 4.625% 4.375% 4.25%
Price $19.04 $18.21 $17.57 $16.70 $16.42
Yield 6.6% 6.6% 6.6% 6.6% 6.2%
Call date 12/1/24 6/1/25 12/1/25 9/1/26 9/1/26
YTC NA NA NA 67% 68%

All are rated BB, eligible for the preferential tax rate but dividends are non-cumulative. Both the Series L & N can be Called early if they become Tier 1 capital ineligible, but their low coupons make that extremely unlikely: I would ignore their attractive YTC values

  • L yields more and has the same Call date than N. That said, with the lowest coupon, N should be Called last.
  • Of the three currently Callable, K would be the best choice as it has the lowest coupon within this set. That said, it would be Called before either L or N.

With L’s yield advantage over N and lower coupon versus the rest with similar yields, I’m giving the Buy rating to L. When compared to several large national banks, this issue provides 50-60bps in extra yield.

Portfolio strategy

Along with evaluating risks (interest rate, default, rating downgrade), Call risk needs attention too, especially for fixed-rate issues during periods of declining interest rates. It means looking beyond what the FOMC is doing, which mostly affects short-term rates to inflation expectations, a driver effecting longer term rates.

The first chart show the forecast for the FFR. The second chart shows the inflation forecast.

Forecasts show the FFR bottoming out in 2027 as inflation finally reaches the 2% FOMC target level.

There are strategies to reduce the Call risk. The first and most obvious is owning issues not Callable for years, although five years is the maximum protection I’ve seen, though there are perpetual issues. The second and potentially longer protection can come from owning coupons that are toward the lower end of what an issuer has outstanding as logic says the higher coupon issues should get Called first. Another strategy, which comes with its own set of risks, that being owning issues from an issuer too weak to Call any of their preferred stocks.

data.title: JPMorgan: AI Productivity Gains Make It Worth Premium
data.description:
Introduction

When JPMorgan (JPM) reported for its most recent fiscal quarter (Q3 2025), I was one of the first analysts on Seeking Alpha to update my investment coverage on the stock. I kept my "Buy" rating in place in mid-October because the slight post-earnings dip at the time seemed a bit overdone to me and fully unjustified. At least the fundamentals of the bank didn't show any red signs and flags, which might have suggested that JPM deserves a negative perception after its Q3 print. It's been over 2 months since my update, and the JPM stock keeps beating the S&P 500 index (SP500) (SPX) (SPY), albeit slightly. And as banks usually report first, we're actually rapidly approaching JPM's Q4 print, which is set to be released in about 3 weeks. I'd like to revisit my previous rating and see what's coming for Q4, trying to answer whether it's worth buying JPM ahead of the new earnings season next month.

What's New Since My Q3 Note

While I covered a bunch of points in my October piece, we've actually gotten 2 more important datapoints that sharpen my previous bullish stance.

First, the sell-side has moved its estimates after the Q3 earnings report for each of the next 8 years in terms of EPS expansion. In some cases, the uplifts were massive, at double-digits, based on Seeking Alpha:

And it couldn't help but lead to yet another round of price target bullish adjustments. The current PT consensus gives JPM a modest 1.7% upside potential from today's price, but the trend itself looks intact:

Seeking Alpha's above chart clearly shows that JPM usually trades at a narrow discount to the Street's price target, so anyway, the current consensus PT might not be fully representative of what's coming next, in my view.

Moreover, there are analysts who view JPM as a solid bullish pick for 2026. For example, Argus Research analysts (proprietary source, October 2025) lifted their PT to $340/share from $318 they previously had, and they also raised FY2026 EPS estimate to $20.66. It's a solid 5.26% upside, not counting a) potential bottom-line beats along the way and b) the dividend yield, which is almost at 2% for FY2026.

The second important thing that I didn't cover in October - and I couldn'tphysically do it back then - was the recently announced incremental spend on top of the existing $95.9 billion 2025 adjusted expense guide I touched upon previously.

So Jeremy mentioned that consensus was a little low, and we have seen it move up slightly since third quarter. I just want to be sort of kind of clear with you guys that we finished -- largely finished, I would say, our second round of budgeting for 2026. I think we have a reasonable line of sight obviously dependent on scenarios and everything else, but we're expecting our expenses for the full firm next year to be $105 billion.

Source: JPM at Goldman Sachs' conference [December 2025]

The extended expense is set to boost JPM's durable productivity, which could lead to a lower required headcount growth over the next years. As the bank keeps spending a lot to pull forward share gains in the highest-ROIC franchises like cards, wealth, payments, and IB, I believe the additional $9.1 billion (+9.5% to what was expected right after Q3) is a clear bullish catalyst for JPM's forward years' margins. That's likely why we saw that many positive revisions across the board. Last time I looked at JPM's strategy, I was viewing their new "innovation-driving" expenses as investments for a better future, and now the management basically pointed to a) more investments, and b) a clear 3-bucket explanation of how they're supposed to reach the above-mentioned productivity gains over time.

The biggest bucket of growth for us in expense is volume and growth-related expenses, which, to me, are high-quality expenses associated with our growth strategies and outperforming things like performance, incentive compensation for advisers or the product marketing expense as we are refreshing cards and having people engage more strongly on them or auto lease, which, as you know, gets grossed up in terms of accounting and has a meaningful impact on expense when we see outsized growth which we have and expect to continue into 2026.

But the second biggest driver is our strategic investments, which will be incredibly familiar to you. They have very predictable and strong returns.

And then the third bucket, which is the smallest, but nevertheless, not nothing is the sort of structural consequence of inflation and real estate costs and everything else.

Source: JPM at Goldman Sachs' conference

Now, if we look at JPM's valuation, it becomes logical why the market is willing to overpay for the right to own the stock. The 5-year average P/E multiple of JPM stock is ~12x, according to Seeking Alpha data. The FY2026 P/E ratio stands at 15.25x, so it's 27% higher than the long-term average.

But JPM had an effective 10-year CAGR in diluted EPS of ~12.89%, while now, with all the efficiencies on the horizon, the current consensus expects ~8.34% CAGR for the next 9 years:

I think the actual growth is likely to exceed that new long-term growth target that comes from consensus data. JPM is likely to keep beating, at least for the next few quarters, because the investments it's been making for months are already translating into real gains. For example, at Goldman's conference that I referred to above, JPM's representative noted ~6% improvement in productivity as some of the digital assistance & personalization investments are paying off in real time.

We expected to increase the productivity of our ops specialists by more than 40% so let's say, 40% to 50% growth. So we're a growth business. We're going to grow through a lot of that. So the net headcount reduction will be lower, but each individual operator will be, call it, 50% more productive. And we're seeing that. So we've seen the step change required to deliver that this year. So whereas we were improving that productivity at, call it, an average 3-ish percent a year previously, largely not AI-enabled, we've now doubled that to 6% and expect that to be maybe not a hockey stick, but to improve. We're like well on our way. It is very real. It is working. It is happening. And it's a combination of work elimination through self-service investments like digital assistance and personalization, it's process automation around things like document management or AI voice.

Source: JPM at Goldman Sachs' conference, emphasis added

With that in mind, JPM is very likely to show a similar period of superior consensus beating that we saw throughout the 2019-2022 period:

At an average premium of 3-4% to the current consensus in the next few years, JPM's P/E ratio will quickly reach the 5-year average, assuming no change in the stock price. But again, I think that the premium is here to stay with us, so JPM is likely to adjust higher with the above-consensus rise in its earnings. At 15-16x earnings and 4% premium on top of today's FY2026 EPS figure, JPM's 12-month price target is $344.7/share, which is 6.7% above the current price. After adding ~2% in dividend yield, the total return potential comes in at a high single-digit percentage, which is good for the biggest bank in the world.

Risks

The two main caveats that I see putting my bullish thesis to the test are valuation and JPM's future operating leverage. JPM is already priced above 15x forward earnings, and so the upside potential that I come up with (6.7%) may not be sufficient for value investors for the level of risks that they're taking today. In case JPM's NII growth undershoots today's the grow-through-the-cuts narrative, and their incremental ~$9.1B spend does not translate into measurable productivity and fee growth, we may not see any meaningful EPS growth at all. The operating leverage may not help, and the JPM stock would contract by 10-20% in that case, easily.

Conclusion

Despite the existing risks, I think that JPM is a nice pick among large banks for 2026. Yes, it's quite pricey, but the level of execution at the helm still looks superb. I believe the market is paying the right price for JPM today, and the stock can gain 6.7% in nominal terms over the next 12 months if my math is correct. I leave my "Buy" rating with no changes today.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: Buy The Blue Chip: Ares Capital Is A 9.6% Yielding Bargain
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving.

Have you ever loaned money to a friend or family member? Once, I had a friend who borrowed a bit of money from me while we were on a trip. It was a bit of an awkward situation because we were on a trip together, and suddenly he didn't have enough money, and I wasn't ready to go home.

After we got home, I didn't see or hear from this "friend" for three years. He avoided my calls and actively avoided the mutual places we used to go to. Losing the money is one thing, but having someone that I had thought of as a friend ghost me over a few bucks hurt worse. If he had just maintained communication, I probably would have forgiven the debt anyway.

It's just a reality of life that even people you think are very reliable sometimes aren't. I'm hardly the only person with a story of a friend or family member who was duped on a debt.

If people are willing to destroy relationships with people over money and stiff their friends and family, should we be surprised that they don't always pay their debt to corporate strangers? Of course not.

The reality of lending money to anyone for any reason, whether you do it directly to someone you know well or indirectly through an investment in a company that makes loans, is that some people won't pay you back. Some people will lie, cheat, or steal, and some might make an honest attempt to pay it back, but for whatever reason, are incapable of paying you back.

Despite this, being a lender is a fantastic way to generate cash flow. Today, we're going to talk about a company that has a history of great judgment in lending, but more importantly, when borrowers default, management has a knack for turning it into an opportunity.

ARCC – Yield 9.6%

Ares Capital Corporation (ARCC) is one of the largest, oldest, and highest-quality business development companies, or BDCs, that is publicly traded. ARCC is currently trading at par to NAV, where historically we have often seen it trade at premiums of 10-20%. Whenever we can buy ARCC at just a small premium, we are happy to snag some more shares.

The last time we wrote about ARCC, we noted that it had just reported a new record NAV per share. It continues to trade at a similar price as the market continues to be bogged down in the narrative about the "dangers" of private credit.

If you've been watching the financial news, you've likely seen some ominous articles warning about the "dangers" of "private credit." The ironic part is that the big defaults that have made the headlines were deals that were underwritten by banks. Tricolor and First Brands were deals that were underwritten by and primarily held by banks.

Banks Becoming Standardized

Now we don't intend to throw banks under the bus. When you lend money, borrowers will default. That's just the reality of life, whether you're lending money to your kids, your best friend, coworkers, or companies. Borrowers don't always pay back as agreed, and that's why interest rates on loans are higher than the interest rate on US Treasuries. The interest rate has to be higher to compensate for the risk of not being paid back.

Banks don't have some secret formula to avoid that reality. Nor are they necessarily any better than anyone else at assessing it. In fact, banks have really standardized their lending processes. You go apply for that credit card and get an approval or denial within 5 seconds... guess what? No human looked at your application. Nobody verified your income. The computer pulled your credit report, verified a handful of metrics were within whatever their predetermined ranges are, and spit out a result.

The bank doesn't really care if you, in particular, pay your credit card bill. They are going for volume, knowing that X% of people who fit Y demographic with Z credit score range are going to default, and they are ok with that default rate because they are charging enough interest to make up for it from the people who do pay their credit card bills.

The standardization has benefits. It makes credit widely available to people and removes the risk of human error and biases from the process. The problem is that with a business, your lending needs might be a bit more complex than what can comfortably fit within the standardization of bank lending. Many companies have seasonal ebbs and flows; they have unique needs, goals, and collateral. It really is a form of lending that requires more judgment and more human decision-making than a credit card or auto loan.

BDCs Provide Custom Options

This is where BDCs come in. BDCs are far more flexible than banks. A BDC has permanent capital, which means that it never has to worry about depositors withdrawing money. Banks always have to remain somewhat liquid because depositors can demand their cash at any time without warning. As a result, banks are somewhat constrained in the number of illiquid loans they can carry on their balance sheet, and they have little to no interest in holding physical assets or equity.

BDCs have the luxury of permanent capital, meaning that they are never in a hurry to liquidate their assets. They can hold stock in private companies, they can accept PIK (payment in-kind) payments in lieu of cash, and they can step in and actively assist in managing the business. When tough times hit, a bank is going to just collect its money, file lawsuits, and push to recover whatever with speed as a major part of its priority. A BDC can craft a plan that leads to the maximum possible recovery.

Some BDCs do that better than others. ARCC is amongst the best. Out of +3,115 First-Lien investments since IPO, ARCC has sector-leading credit losses of just 0.1%: Source.

That chart is for years 2004 through Q2 2025, a period that includes the GFC and COVID. It's a fraction of what was lost by leveraged loans, which are originated by banks. The 2nd lien loans predictably did worse, but 0.2% is a fantastic number.

But the best part is that, over those 20 years, ARCC has realized net gains on its par value of over $1 billion. A gain rate of approximately 1% on the par value of its investments after accounting for realized credit losses.

When a borrower gets distressed, for ARCC, that is often an opportunity to make more money rather than realize a loss. It's something that banks aren't really capable of doing because they aren't going to take minority equity positions that might pay off in the future. The capital structure of banks forces them to keep relatively liquid assets.

Conclusion

While other BDCs could do the same thing ARCC does, the reality is that not all BDCs are equal. Some BDCs do better than others when it comes to managing risk and avoiding defaults. Some do better than others in making lemonade out of it when borrowers do default. The team at Ares Management (ARES) is one of the most elite groups in the world at managing these situations.

Are banks better than BDCs at underwriting? Well, some banks might be better than some BDCs. The capital structure of a BDC is an advantage, but it still has to be tempered with responsible underwriting and a team that has the ability to resolve problems. We've seen some BDCs run into credit troubles and just hold a fire sale, permanently losing value rather than finding an alternative solution that might recover value in the future.

Is ARCC better at underwriting and navigating credit issues than banks and the average BDC? Yes. ARCC has a long track record of low default rates and exceptionally low loss rates, as it has negotiated deals with upside to more than offset the downside realized in other holdings. More impressively, ARCC has managed to do this through periods of significant economic turmoil like the GFC and COVID-19.

That's why we're buying ARCC. This is a BDC we are happy to hold through economic turmoil, confident that management can navigate to the best outcome rather than just blindly cutting losses when borrowers are distressed.

data.title: Global X Silver Miners ETF: Leveraged Exposure To Silver As The Cycle Extends Into 2026
data.description:
Currently silver occupies a central place in the market, since, in recent months, the metal has been reaching historical highs, accompanied by gold also breaking records. And I do not consider it to be a one-off movement in prices, but how the market sees the macro forward.

What does the market see today? As the following SA article explains very well, lower rates are expected, a less dominant dollar and a still tense geopolitical context:

Traders are betting the Federal Reserve will cut interest rates twice in 2026 after a host of economic data last week, while U.S. President Donald Trump has also advocated for looser monetary policy.

Elsewhere, rising geopolitical tensions in recent weeks have further strengthened the safe-haven appeal of gold and silver. The United States has tightened its oil blockade on Venezuela, increasing pressure on President Nicolas Maduro’s government, while Ukraine has, for the first time, attacked an oil tanker belonging to Russia’s shadow fleet in the Mediterranean Sea.

An extra point to highlight is that silver usually behaves differently from gold. It maintains its role as a safe-haven asset, also facing a relevant industrial demand, which explains why, when the cycle is activated, silver tends to move more intensively.

When that happens, the miners tend to amplify the movement. With largely fixed costs, metal price changes directly impact margins and cash generation, which is where Global X Silver Miners ETF (SIL) comes in as a simple way to capture that operational leverage without having to select a single company.

What kind of exposure SIL offers?

SIL is a very direct ETF, since almost all the fund is invested in companies in the basic materials sector, mainly mining linked to silver. The level of cash is minimal and there are no defensive assets to cushion the cycles.

Although the ETF has more than 40 positions, the portfolio is quite concentrated: the top ten account for about 75% of the total, which in practice means that the fund’s performance depends largely on a few companies. There are primary silver producers, diversified companies and some royalty firms that bring some more relative stability, as we see in the table below, but the profile is still clearly cyclical.

This structure explains why the ETF tends to move more than the metal itself, both up and down.

The recent rally and what the numbers show

When I look at the momentum, I see that the recent movement of the ETF was strong. In the last month, SIL rose about 25%, and in the last 6 months, the advance exceeds 70%. In year-to-year terms, the rise is around 160%, well above the S&P 500 in the same period.

In the longer term, the pattern is maintained, although with an irregular path. In 3 years, the ETF accumulates increases close to 220%. It is not a background of smooth trajectories, but of marked cycles and high volatility.

Beyond price, some data helps put the rally in context. The companies that integrate the ETF show an ROE close to 12%, with an average capitalization of almost $19B. At the same time, multiples are being adjusted as results improve, with the P/E ratio falling from very high levels to more reasonable values.

This does not eliminate the risk, but suggests that some of the recent advance is also accompanied by improvements in fundamentals and not just euphoria.

How much can SIL be worth in 2026?

Thinking about 2026, I estimated three scenarios, using as a reference the price of silver and the historical behavior of mining companies, rather than external target prices.

If silver corrected to lower levels, the impact on mining would be rapid. On the other hand, I believe that if it manages to consolidate at high levels for longer, the effect on margins and cash generation may be more durable and sustainable.

From that approach, I got these price ranges for SIL in 2026, based on a current price close to $88.

In a more conservative scenario, with silver down to the $55-60/oz zone, the impact on mining would be rapid. Given the operational leverage of the sector, a drop in the metal is usually amplified in earnings and stock price, which could lead SIL to move in an approximate range of $70-80.

My base scenario for 2026 assumes that silver consolidates between $65 and $70 per ounce. In that context, I do not expect a new explosive rally, but a more gradual extension of the cycle, as I estimate that with sustained metal prices and still healthy margins, SIL could place itself in a $90-100 range.

In a more bullish scenario, with silver surpassing $75 per ounce, I believe that operational leverage would play back in mining’s favor. If that level is maintained over a prolonged period, the ETF would have room to look for higher levels, in the $110-120 area, although with high volatility along the way.

Risks

The main risk I observe is a correction in the price of silver. Given the operational leverage of mining companies, any relevant metal decline is usually amplified in the ETF.

Operational and political risks in some mining jurisdictions must also be considered, as well as the portfolio concentration itself.

Moreover, after such a strong rally, periods of consolidation or corrections cannot be ruled out, even in the current context.

Conclusion

From my point of view, SIL remains an efficient tool to expose yourself to the silver cycle through mining, in a context where metal is kept at historically high levels and the macro environment remains supportive. The ETF does exactly what it promises: amplifies metal movements.

At the same time, much of the initial movement has already occurred, and from these levels the return risk profile is positive, but more balanced. For investors who understand the volatility of the sector and maintain a constructive view on silver towards 2026, my stance is Buy, as I consider that SIL continues to make sense within a diversified portfolio.

data.title: Aeluma: When A Semiconductor Story Stops Being A Science Project
data.description:
A year ago this was a science project. Today, it looks like a company preparing to execute.

Author's Note:

During much of 2025, I have conducted a deep, ongoing diligence process on Aeluma (ALMU) to understand not only its technology but also its evolving commercial posture. My interest stems from how rarely investors encounter a public semiconductor company at this stage: one built on years of non-dilutive government funding, validated technology, and a growing roster of sophisticated customers — yet still early enough that the market has not fully adjusted its expectations.

Aeluma operates at the intersection of compound semiconductors, photonics, and advanced manufacturing, with potential applications spanning aerospace and defense, AI infrastructure, quantum, consumer devices, and wearables. As I noted in my earlier 2025 articles (Prati - ALMU article 1) and (Prati - ALMU article 2), the technical foundation has long been compelling. What has changed more recently is the company’s posture: Aeluma is transitioning from a research-driven organization into an execution-focused commercial enterprise.

Founder and CEO Jonathan Klamkin brings deep technical credibility and is now demonstrating increasing operational leadership as the company moves toward design wins and early commercial revenue. For investors willing to do the work, Aeluma represents a rare opportunity to study — and potentially own — a company at the moment when technical risk begins to give way to execution risk. These moments are often understated in headlines, but they are where long-term value creation typically begins. The Aeluma story has quietly changed from "interesting" to "inevitable."

Why Tone Matters More Than Headlines

In early 2025, Aeluma was widely perceived as an R&D-heavy photonics developer — technologically impressive, but commercially uncertain.

That perception was not irrational. Qualification timelines were long, customer programs were still exploratory, and management communication leaned deliberately conservative. Investors were asked, repeatedly, to be patient.

What has changed over the past nine months is not a single announcement or headline-grabbing press release. It is the consistency and direction of management tone across multiple touchpoints, combined with observable shifts in customer behavior. In semiconductors, these qualitative signals often precede financial inflection by several quarters. Markets are generally poor at pricing such transitions — even though they often mark the most attractive entry points.

At the center of this shift is a manufacturing platform that quietly moved Aeluma from technical validation toward commercial relevance.

September 2025: Maximum Conservatism

The FY2026 outlook provided in early September was notably restrained.

Management emphasized:

  • Long qualification timelines
  • Customer diligence and testing cycles
  • The distinction between engagement and commercialization

This posture was appropriate — and arguably necessary — given Aeluma’s history and the broader skepticism surrounding the commercialization of photonics.

In hindsight, however, that call marked the low point in expressed confidence. Not because fundamentals deteriorated, but because the company was transitioning from proof to preparation.

November 12: A Subtle but Meaningful Pivot

By the Q1 earnings call in November, the language had changed.

Not dramatically — but unmistakably.

Management began emphasizing:

  • The number of active customer engagements
  • The maturity of several programs
  • Discussions shifting toward yield, reliability, and manufacturability

Importantly, the conversation moved away from “Does the technology work?” toward “How does this integrate into our roadmap?”

That distinction matters enormously. In semiconductors, integration planning is the precursor to design wins, even if formal announcements lag.

December Fireside Chat: “Go Time” Without the Hype

The Benchmark fireside chat from last Thursday represented a third — and decisive — tonal evolution.

What stood out was not promotional language but operational specificity:

  • Execution cadence replaced long-term vision
  • Customer discussions were framed around timelines and scale
  • Internal focus shifted toward hiring, manufacturing readiness, and throughput

The phrase “it’s go time” was notable not because it was enthusiastic, but because it was matter-of-fact. It reflected urgency rooted in customer pull rather than aspirational optimism.

In academic terms, this looked clearly like a company crossing from technology risk into execution risk — a far more attractive position for long-term capital.

Why This Transition Is Easy to Miss

Aeluma does not yet have:

  • Named hyperscaler design wins
  • Large, visible commercial revenue
  • Clean quarterly inflection points

As a result, the stock continues to trade as though little has changed.

But internally, the company now appears to be operating in a different phase:

  • Customers are allocating engineering resources
  • Manufacturing discussions are active
  • Late-stage engagements are narrowing toward qualification

These are the conditions under which semiconductor businesses quietly re-rate — often before revenue becomes obvious.

The Valuation Disconnect

Even conservative external models imply a materially different company by 2028.

Yet today’s valuation still reflects:

  • Skepticism about commercialization
  • Fatigue from prior dilution
  • Discomfort with long timelines

That disconnect creates an unusual setup: perceived risk is declining faster than its valuation reflects.

For investors with a multi-year horizon, this is precisely where asymmetry tends to emerge.

Why This Is Increasingly a “Tuck-Away” Idea

Aeluma does not need to become a household name in 2026 for the thesis to work.

It needs only to:

  • Convert late-stage engagements into qualification
  • Begin modest commercial ramps
  • Demonstrate repeatability and yield

If those conditions are met, the valuation framework changes — regardless of short-term volatility.

This is not about predicting the next quarter. It is about recognizing when a company has crossed an internal threshold that the market has not yet priced.

A Note for Retail and Institutional Investors Alike

For retail investors, opportunities to buy into companies with validated technology, strong IP, and early commercial momentum — before institutional ownership becomes inevitable — are genuinely rare.

For institutional investors, the hesitation is understandable: Aeluma is small, illiquid, and early. But that is precisely the point. Structural constraints often prevent meaningful exposure during the most asymmetric phase of value creation.

This is not a base-case forecast, but it illustrates the optionality: if Aeluma executes into even a few hundred million dollars of revenue over the coming years, the company’s scale — and market perception — would change dramatically. The largest returns in semiconductors are often captured not by waiting for certainty, but by recognizing transition early.

Final Thought

Markets are excellent at pricing certainty and terrible at pricing transition.

Over the past year, Aeluma has moved from cautious validation toward confident execution — not in headlines, but in behavior, tone, and operational focus.

For investors willing to look past near-term noise, this increasingly resembles the early stages of a multi-year re-rating — the kind best captured by buying early and doing very little afterward.

Author’s Note: This article was written entirely by the author without the use of generative AI tools. All analyses and conclusions are original and based on publicly available information.

data.title: Lowe's: Still Looking For Reasons To Get Excited
data.description:
Lowe's (LOW) shares have yielded fairly underwhelming results over the past five years, significantly lagging behind the market.

From first principles, if I had told you that EPS and revenue would grow at a mid-single-digit rate, with the stock coming into this stretch at a 25x+ P/E multiple, you would probably guess that results would be poor.

Well, I argue that the current situation isn't that much different and that investors should patiently wait for triggers.

Lowe's Past Five Years & Investing First Principles

If I had to narrow down all of my investing lessons into one rule, it would be Peter Lynch's rule that stocks follow earnings growth.

Once you truly understand this rule, the investing analysis process becomes much simpler. You realize that in order to generate market-besting returns, you must identify companies that have under-appreciated earnings growth trajectories.

Such under-appreciation can take the form of a low multiple relative to growth, or growth expectations that are too low, either near-term or long-term.

This is where Buffett's wide moat kicks in. Wide moat companies tend to generate far better growth over the long term than anyone is willing to model out today, which gives them an inherent under-appreciation.

Over the past 5 years, Lowe's stock severely lagged the market, with about 38 points of underperformance.

From the Peter Lynch rule perspective, it's easy to understand why. Despite growing mid-single-digits annually, lower than the market average, Lowe's consistently traded above market-average valuation.

This is precisely the opposite of under-appreciation. There are several explanations for the market being overly optimistic about Lowe's.

The company is historical, has a track record of strong returns, is entrenched in US retail, and it appeals to dividend investors who like its long dividend streak.

Note, neither of those reasons fits the Peter Lynch rule of yielding good returns. Now, the question is, can this happen?

Last Quarter Was More Of The Same

In the recently announced quarter, Lowe's revenue was $20.8 billion, up 3% Y/Y, mainly driven by comps sales of 0.4%, and online sales growth of 11.4%. Dissecting comps, transactions were down 3.0%, while the average ticket was up 3.4%, not the best mix. In addition, comps trended worse and worse from month to month, in line with comps.

EPS was down 3.7% Y/Y, as a 50bps improvement in gross margin was more than offset by higher operating expenses, leading to a 65bps contraction in operating margin. On an adjusted basis, operating margins were up 10 bps, and adjusted EPS was up 5.9% Y/Y.

The adjustments include costs associated with Lowe's acquisitions of Foundation Building Materials and Artisan Design Group, which are aimed at enhancing its offerings, especially for professional customers.

All up, Lowe's once again missed expectations, this time for both revenue and GAAP EPS, making it the second quarter of 2025 with a miss. This takes me back to my over-appreciation comment. As we can see, this isn't the case with Lowe's.

For their near-term outlook, Lowe's reaffirmed or downgraded certain guidance items, which now include the FBM contribution impact.

Comparable sales are now expected to be flat, vs. flat to up 1% previously. Adjusted operating margins are expected to be lower, at 12.1%, and adjusted EPS is expected to be at the low-end of the previous range.

I don't think you can look at the quarter's numbers and sense a beginning of a reignition or find a signal that we're on the verge of acceleration. To me, this is pretty much more of the same.

Lowe's Valuation - Craziness Is Doing The Same Thing And Expecting Different Results

At $240 per share, Lowe's trades at 20 times forward earnings and 19 times next year's estimates.

While this valuation finally places Lowe's below the market average multiple of 22x, this is still quite relative to Lowe's growth prospects.

Consensus sees Lowe's EPS growth accelerating to 8% by 2027. If we take that as the normalized growth rate, the current P/E multiple would take us to a PEG ratio of 2.5x, which is considered very high.

This is a similar situation to the one five years ago, and I don’t see any reason to think it will yield a different result. To me, a 2x PEG, or 16 times earnings, is where Lowe's ultimately becomes interesting.

Potential Triggers

Up until now, we talked about why Lowe's is not an interesting investment, but that's easy. Let's try to identify what could occur that will change that.

I think that by far, the best thing that could happen for Lowe's is a sudden boom in home improvements and home building, two markets that don't always overlap, but sometimes they do.

Right now, whether it's macro, interest rates, tariffs, deportations, or still dealing with the COVID pull-forward, none of those markets are exactly booming.

Existing home sales (a key leading indicator for home improvement) are still around 10-year lows, while housing starts are at 5-year lows.

Until mortgage rates fall low enough so that both these industries reignite, I don't see a path for meaningful acceleration for Lowe's. Even as Lowe's tries to lean more heavily on professionals, it is still sensitive to the same end markets.

In other words, its professional-forward strategy doesn't change the fact that the number one data point I would monitor before entering a position is mortgage rates, as it solves both end markets.

Lower rates allow for more existing home sales, which then allow more new home sales, which then drive more housing starts, and that's the magic flywheel that needs to get going.

Conclusion

Lowe's is a legendary American company that probably won't be disrupted in this lifetime. As safe as it is, equity investors should look for upside, not safety.

I still don't see a justification to buy the stock at this PEG, as the path for market-beating acceleration remains uncertain.

As such, I reiterate a Hold.
